items.title: Binary classifier output neuron
items.scenario: A fraud-detection MLP ends with one linear unit producing z = −0.3, then σ(z) ≈ 0.43 as P(fraud). The ops team flags transactions with ŷ > 0.7 regardless of the default 0.5 training threshold.
items.explanation: Sigmoid on the final layer maps an unbounded score to (0, 1) for probability interpretation. The squashing is appropriate here; vanishing gradients mainly hurt deep hidden stacks, not a single output gate.

items.title: Saturated hidden unit in an old 8-layer MLP
items.scenario: During training, one hidden neuron consistently receives pre-activation x = 6.2, so σ(x) ≈ 0.998 and σ′(x) ≈ 0.002. Weight updates for layers below it shrink by roughly three orders of magnitude compared to a neuron near x = 0.
items.explanation: Upper saturation kills local gradient flow. This is the practical face of vanishing gradients — not abstract math, but a unit stuck on the flat top of the S-curve.

items.title: LSTM forget gate
items.scenario: An LSTM cell computes f_t = σ(W_f · [h_{t−1}, x_t] + b_f). When f_t ≈ 0.9 the cell retains 90% of its previous state; when f_t ≈ 0.1 it mostly resets. Gate values must stay in (0, 1).
items.explanation: Gating needs bounded multiplicative factors. Sigmoid remains a natural choice for gates even when feedforward hidden layers moved to ReLU.

items.title: Neuron near decision boundary
items.scenario: Two inputs produce pre-activations x = −0.1 and x = 0.1. Sigmoid outputs σ(−0.1) ≈ 0.475 and σ(0.1) ≈ 0.525 — only 0.05 apart, with healthy derivatives near 0.25.
items.explanation: Sigmoid is most informative and trainable when pre-activations stay near zero. Feature scaling and initialization help keep units out of saturation during early training.
